metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Literary Studies 11 develops students' capacity for sophisticated literary analysis through engagement with a wide range of complex texts. Students read with growing attention to how language, structure, and context construct meaning; analyze how social, cultural, and historical forces shape texts; and develop nuanced interpretations supported by textual evidence. The course emphasizes deep reading, discussion, and academic writing.
metadata.general_description: A rigorous study of literary texts across genres and traditions, developing sophisticated reading, analysis, and academic writing skills while examining how texts are shaped by and reflect social, cultural, and historical contexts.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: At Grade 11, literary study moves beyond plot comprehension to examine how literature constructs and questions the very ideas through which we understand ourselves and our world.

big_ideas.statement: People understand text differently depending on their world views and perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Interpretation is never neutral — readers' positionality (gender, culture, class, history) shapes what they notice and value in a text, making literary study a site of productive disagreement.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Every text is a historical artifact. Understanding when, where, why, and for whom it was written unlocks deeper layers of meaning.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: At a sophisticated level, students examine how specific word choices, rhetorical moves, and formal decisions do not just convey meaning but actively construct it.

big_ideas.statement: Questioning what we hear, read, and view contributes to our ability to be educated and engaged citizens.
big_ideas.elaboration: Critical literacy at this level means examining the assumptions, ideologies, and power structures embedded in texts — including texts presented as neutral or objective.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Read for enjoyment and to achieve personal goals.
i_can_statements: I can engage with literary texts for personal pleasure and growth.
I can set and reflect on personal reading goals.
I can explain why I am drawn to certain genres, authors, or themes.

competencies.raw: Understand story and oral tradition's role in expressing First Peoples worldviews; recognize diversity within First Peoples societies; understand land/place influence.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how oral tradition functions as a living form of law, history, and cultural transmission.
I can identify the specific nation and cultural context of a First Peoples text and explain why that matters.
I can analyze how a specific territory or place functions symbolically and literally in a First Peoples text.

competencies.raw: Evaluate relevance, accuracy, and reliability of texts; consider credibility, currency, bias.
i_can_statements: I can evaluate secondary sources about a literary text for credibility and relevance.
I can identify the perspective and potential bias of a literary critic or commentator.
I can distinguish between a well-supported literary argument and an unsupported opinion.

competencies.raw: Apply comprehension strategies to written, oral, visual, and multimodal texts; guide inquiry and extend thinking.
i_can_statements: I can apply active reading strategies to challenging literary texts.
I can annotate a text for language, structure, and literary devices.
I can extend my analysis beyond the literal by examining subtext, tone, and implied meaning.

competencies.raw: Think critically and creatively; recognize personal, social, cultural contexts; understand how language constructs identities; evaluate literary elements; discern nuanced word meanings; identify bias, contradictions, distortions, omissions.
i_can_statements: I can analyze how a text positions the reader to accept certain values or assumptions.
I can discuss how gender, race, class, or sexuality shape how a character is represented.
I can identify what is absent or silenced in a text and explain the significance of that absence.
I can analyze connotative and denotative meanings of specific word choices in a literary text.
I can evaluate how literary elements (structure, voice, imagery, symbolism) create specific effects.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Respectfully exchange ideas and viewpoints from diverse perspectives using active listening, paraphrasing, respectful disagreement, and collaborative engagement.
i_can_statements: I can participate in Socratic seminars and literary discussions with genuine intellectual engagement.
I can paraphrase a classmate's literary argument and respond to its substance, not a distortion.
I can disagree respectfully with a peer's interpretation and support my own view with evidence.

competencies.raw: Respond to texts personally, creatively, and critically; demonstrate speaking and listening skills in formal and informal contexts.
i_can_statements: I can write a nuanced personal response that goes beyond surface-level reaction.
I can create an imaginative response to a text that demonstrates deep understanding.
I can present a literary analysis with confidence, clarity, and appropriate academic register.

competencies.raw: Use writing and design processes; express and support opinions with evidence; refine texts through creative manipulation of language and intentional stylistic choices.
i_can_statements: I can write a well-structured analytical essay with a clear argument, textual evidence, and commentary.
I can refine my literary writing using intentional stylistic choices — diction, syntax, structure.
I can use fragments, inverted syntax, or other non-standard choices deliberately and effectively.

competencies.raw: Apply Canadian spelling, grammar, and punctuation conventions; use citations; recognize intellectual property and First Peoples protocols; transform ideas to create original texts.
i_can_statements: I can apply grammar and punctuation conventions consistently in academic writing.
I can cite literary sources accurately using MLA or another accepted format.
I can create original texts — analytical, creative, or multimodal — that demonstrate sophisticated engagement with literature.

areas.name: Text Forms, Genres, Features and Structures
areas.raw: Forms and genres of texts; visual/graphic text elements; narrative structures found in First Peoples texts; protocols related to ownership of First Peoples oral texts.
topics: Novel, short story, novella — extended literary analysis
Drama: stage, film, and radio
Poetry: lyric, narrative, dramatic, experimental, spoken word
Creative non-fiction: memoir, essay, literary journalism
Graphic texts and visual literature
First Peoples texts: contemporary and traditional, oral and written
Narrative structures: linear, circular, iterative, episodic, non-linear
Point of view: reliability, unreliable narrators, multiple perspectives

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies; oral language strategies; metacognitive strategies; writing processes; design processes.
topics: Close reading and textual annotation
Identifying theme, symbol, motif, and leitmotif
Comparative literary analysis across texts
Socratic seminar preparation and participation
Academic essay writing: thesis, argument, evidence, commentary
Metacognitive reflection on reading and writing growth

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style; usage and conventions; citation techniques; literary elements and devices; literal and inferential meaning.
topics: Diction: connotation, denotation, register, precision
Syntax: sentence variety, rhythm, and rhetorical effect
Tone, mood, and atmosphere: how they are created and their effect
Literary devices: extended metaphor, allegory, symbolism, irony, allusion, intertextuality
Narrative techniques: focalization, free indirect discourse, stream of consciousness
Literal and inferential meaning: reading between and beyond the lines
Academic conventions: thesis statements, topic sentences, citations
